grammar.point: Present Perfect and Present Perfect Continuous
examples: I have read books about dodos.
I have been reading this book for an hour.
They've been playing basketball since three o'clock.
I've been learning English for four years.
rules: Use the present perfect to say what you've done in your life up to now.
Use the present perfect continuous to say that you started doing something in the past and you're still doing it now.
The present perfect continuous uses: have/has + been + verb-ing.

examples: Tigers go to the river every day.
Tigers are going to the river to drink.
Tigers went to the river yesterday.
People have cleared forests to grow crops.
The animals' habitat has suffered.
Tigers have always lived in thick jungles.
rules: Simple Present: use for habits and facts.
Present Continuous: use for actions happening now.
Simple Past: use for completed past actions.
Present Perfect: use for things that happened in the past but have an effect now, or started in the past and continue now.

topic: The Cavity and Have You Any Idea?

examples: I could read by the time I was four.
I couldn't chew after I got a cavity.
Next year, I will be able to ride my bike to school.
I won't be able to drive a car.
rules: Use could and couldn't to say what you did or didn't know how to do in the past.
The future of can/can't is will/won't be able to.
Use will be able to for future ability.

examples: It must be a prosimian. It has comb-like lower teeth.
It has to be a spider. It has eight legs.
That can't be a spider! It only has four legs.
It might be a spider, but I'm not sure.
rules: Use must or has to to say you're sure that something is true.
Use can't to say you're sure that something isn't true.
Use might if you're not sure that something is true.

grammar.point: Future with Will and Going to
examples: The sun will rise at 6:05 tomorrow.
Wheels will always be a central part of life.
I'm going to visit my uncle next week.
Look at those big, black clouds. It's going to rain.
rules: Use will to talk about facts in the future or to make predictions about things you believe will be true.
Use going to to talk about future plans or to make predictions about things you see.
Will is used for future facts and predictions based on belief; going to is used for future plans and predictions based on what you see.

examples: We are meeting our friends at 2:00 on Friday.
The moon will rise at ten o'clock tonight.
Things will never be the same again.
I'm going to take a music class next year.
Look at those clouds. We're going to get wet.
rules: You can use the present continuous to talk about future arrangements, especially when you mention a specific time or place.
Use will for future facts and predictions about things you believe will be true.
Use going to for future plans and predictions about things you see.

examples: "It's a beautiful day," said Magdi. → Magdi said that it was a beautiful day.
"I'm working," said Tom. → Tom said that he was working.
He said that he was going fishing.
She said that she didn't like cats.
rules: Reported speech tells what someone said by using the words said that.
If the direct speech is in the present tense, the reported speech changes to the past tense.
Add said that and change the verb to past tense when reporting what someone said.

grammar.point: Reported Speech with Told or Asked
examples: He told us that Kanzi was learning new things every day.
A classmate asked the scientist what Kanzi was learning.
My teacher told me that Kanzi was learning 450 words.
I asked how many words Kanzi was learning.
rules: Use said that to report what someone said.
You can also use told or asked to report speech.
After told or asked, you need to add an object pronoun or someone's name.
If the direct speech is in the present tense, the reported speech changes to past tense.

bigQuestion: What do different cultures give to the world?

examples: Who saw Finn?
Who did Finn see?
Finn's mom saw Finn.
Finn didn't see anybody.
rules: Ask questions with who, where, when, why, what, which, and how.
Be careful: Who saw Finn? (Who is the subject.) vs. Who did Finn see? (Who is the object.)
When who is the subject, do not use do/does/did in the question.

bigQuestion: What do different cultures give to the world?

examples: Is there a chocolate shop near here? Yes, there is.
Do you like my new sneakers? Yes, I do!
Has it stopped raining? No, it hasn't.
Are you 15 years old? No, I'm not!
rules: Short answers include: Yes, I am; No, he didn't; Yes, they can; No, we won't; Yes, there is.
Using a short answer is more polite than answering only Yes or No.
Short answers repeat the auxiliary verb from the question.

examples: People find corn in the mountains. → Corn is found in the mountains.
Do people grow rice in Canada? → Is rice grown in Canada?
Pizza is eaten in many countries.
Mountains are called the water towers of the world.
rules: We can say the same thing in two different ways by making active or passive sentences.
Using the passive is a good idea if you don't know exactly who or what does something.
Form the passive with: subject + is/are + past participle.

examples: Someone broke my mom's favorite vase. → My mom's favorite vase was broken.
Someone showed the weather forecasts on TV. → The weather forecasts were shown on TV.
Mount Everest was climbed for the first time in 1953.
Last year, a big party was organized in my town.
rules: You can use the active or the passive to say the same thing in two different ways.
You can also do this in the past tense.
Form the past passive with: subject + was/were + past participle.

topic: The Sound of Money and the Smell of Soup

examples: A terrifying man with huge muscles opened the door.
"Three gold coins!" repeated the man aggressively.
The Hodja was reading a newspaper, as he often did.
The little boy greedily ate all the grapes.
rules: Adjectives tell you more about nouns.
Adverbs tell you more about verbs. An adverb of manner tells you how something happens.
An adverb of frequency tells you how often something happens.

examples: If you had paper money, you wouldn't need so many coins.
If I had a bike, I would get to school quicker.
If my friend could fly a plane, we would go to the North Pole.
If I won a prize, I would be very happy.
rules: Use the unreal conditional to talk about things that aren't possible now.
Form: If + past tense, subject + would + verb.
The situation is imaginary or unlikely in the present or future.

examples: The sculptures look bumpy and rough.
I felt sick this morning, but I'm OK now.
You look happy!
This lemon smells good, but it tastes bitter.
Your new song sounds fantastic!
rules: Sense verbs (look, feel, sound, smell, taste) are followed by an adjective, not an adverb.
Do not use an adverb after a sense verb.
Examples of sense verbs: look, feel, sound, smell, taste.

examples: It's difficult to save the passengers and crew.
It's fun to climb a tree.
It's easy to learn English if you read a lot of books.
It's fun to act in a play!
rules: Use it's + adjective + infinitive as subject to express an opinion.
The infinitive (to + verb) acts as the subject of the sentence.
This structure avoids starting a sentence with a verb-ing subject.

grammar.point: A, An, The, and No Article
examples: Before a building can be built, the building needs to be designed.
It's a kangaroo.
It's in the pool.
I play tennis. I saw Jim at lunch. We went to Spain. They like math.
rules: Use a or an the first time you talk about something. Use the when you talk about it again.
Use a or an with things that aren't special (not a specific one).
Use the when the person you're talking to knows what you're talking about.
Don't use an article to talk about sports, meals, most countries, and school subjects.

grammar.point: Review of Tenses and Future Forms
examples: I needed to find the coin.
They had to solve the mystery.
I like castles with turrets.
We need the other half of the coin.
Are you going to explore Peril Castle?
I will see the castles of Wales someday.
rules: Use different tenses and future forms to describe different times.
Past: use simple past or past forms (needed, had to) for completed past actions.
Present: use simple present (like, need) for habits and facts.
Future: use going to for plans and will for predictions and facts.
